name: Arabic (Buckwalter transliteration)

ancestors.notes: The same language and the same phonology as `ar`. Buckwalter re-encodes the Arabic SCRIPT; it does not change the language.

notes: Buckwalter is NOT an orthography — it is a lossless 1:1 ASCII re-encoding of Arabic letters, devised so Arabic could be handled by ASCII-only tooling. Nobody reads Buckwalter as a language; it is a machine convenience, and it is recorded here as `orthography_kind: transliteration` to say so. Contrast Pinyin, which people genuinely read and write and which has a standards body. Buckwalter has none — that absence is the tell. It is supported because consumers hold Arabic corpora in ASCII, and because a transliteration is, structurally, just another grapheme map. INPUT CONTRACT — INHERITED, NOT ESCAPED: Buckwalter encodes the short-vowel diacritics when they are present (a i u F N K ~ o), so VOCALIZED Buckwalter is exactly as transcribable as vocalized Arabic, and UNVOCALIZED Buckwalter is exactly as untranscribable. Re-encoding a script cannot recover information the script never wrote down. The mapping is generated from `ar`'s own grapheme map through the Buckwalter table, so the two can never drift apart. CANONICAL ORDER: like the Arabic script it re-encodes, this spec expects the canonical diacritic order — consonant, then shadda, then vowel (mueal~im, not muEali~m). A re-encoding inherits the source script's conventions, ordering included.
